_purpose: Micro-Migration Network Map for the AL Black Belt cluster (Dallas, Marengo, Sumter, Greene, Perry, Hale, Wilcox, Lowndes counties). Documents the migration corridors that populated, depopulated, and transformed the Black Belt from 1817 to 1960. The defining migration pattern is violent displacement followed by violent exploitation: Creek and Choctaw removal opened the land, the domestic slave trade filled it with enslaved labor, and the Great Migration emptied it when freedom and wages beckoned from the North.
display_name: AL Black Belt — Micro-Migration Network Map

migration_corridors.corridor_name: Creek and Choctaw Removal: The Dispossession That Created the Black Belt
migration_corridors.destination_area: Indian Territory (present-day Oklahoma) via forced removal; some Creek displaced to Florida and Louisiana
migration_corridors.scale: The Creek Nation lost approximately 5 million acres in the Treaty of Fort Jackson (1814) and subsequent cessions. The Choctaw lost their remaining Alabama lands in the Treaty of Dancing Rabbit Creek (1830). Thousands of Native people were dispossessed — killed, removed to Indian Territory, or driven into marginal lands. The eastern Black Belt counties (Dallas, Perry, Lowndes, Wilcox) were Creek cession land. The western counties (Marengo, Sumter, Greene) were Choctaw cession land.

evidence.treaty_records: Treaty of Fort Jackson (1814), Treaty of Washington (1826), Treaty of Cusseta (1832) — Creek cessions. Treaty of Dancing Rabbit Creek (1830) — Choctaw cession. Land office records at the National Archives document the transfer of Native lands to American settlers.
evidence.census_evidence: Creek and Choctaw census rolls taken before removal document families and communities that existed on the land before dispossession. The 1832 Creek census and the Choctaw removal rolls are held at the National Archives.
evidence.land_records: General Land Office (GLO) entry records for the Black Belt counties document the first American purchases on cession lands — many within months of treaty ratification.

chain_mechanics.description: This was not voluntary migration — it was forced removal at gunpoint. The Creek War of 1813-14 ended with Andrew Jackson imposing the Treaty of Fort Jackson, which ceded 23 million acres of Creek land. Subsequent treaties stripped remaining Creek and Choctaw lands. Creek families who attempted to remain were subjected to fraud, violence, and starvation until removal to Indian Territory. The Choctaw removal under Dancing Rabbit Creek included some provisions for individuals to remain and receive allotments, but fraud by white speculators stripped most allottees of their land within a decade.
chain_mechanics.kinship_role: Creek and Choctaw communities were organized by clan and kinship networks that were deliberately disrupted by removal. Families were separated. Kinship networks that had governed the land for centuries were shattered.
chain_mechanics.timeline: Creek cession 1814 (eastern counties opened). Choctaw cession 1830 (western counties opened). Forced removal largely complete by 1838.

migration_corridors.dignity_note: The Black Belt was not 'unsettled' or 'wilderness' when American planters arrived. It was the homeland of Creek and Choctaw peoples who were dispossessed by military force, fraudulent treaties, and systematic removal. Every acre of Black Belt cotton land was taken from someone.
migration_corridors.narrative_hook: The land {ancestor_name} farmed in {county} County was Creek territory within living memory — the people who had lived there for centuries were removed by force so that cotton could replace them, and the dark prairie soil did not remember its previous owners in any language the new ones could read.
migration_corridors.source: Rogers et al., Alabama: The History of a Deep South State (1994). Ethridge, Robbie. Creek Country: The Creek Indians and Their World. University of North Carolina Press, 2003.

migration_corridors.corridor_name: Carolina and Georgia Planter Migration with Enslaved Labor
migration_corridors.destination_area: Dallas, Marengo, Sumter, Greene, Perry, Hale, Wilcox, Lowndes — the entire Black Belt
migration_corridors.scale: Hundreds of planter families, each bringing dozens to hundreds of enslaved people. The migration was concentrated in the 1820s-1830s. A single planter family from the Carolina Piedmont or Georgia cotton belt might bring fifty to two hundred enslaved individuals on a forced march lasting weeks.

evidence.census_evidence: 1830 and 1840 census for Black Belt counties shows household heads born in Virginia, the Carolinas, and Georgia. The enslaved population totals in these counties grew explosively between 1820 and 1840 — far faster than natural increase — documenting the massive forced importation of enslaved labor.
evidence.church_evidence: Baptist and Methodist church transfer letters document planter families moving from Carolina and Georgia congregations to newly formed Black Belt congregations.
evidence.surname_clustering: Carolina and Georgia planter surnames dominate the early Black Belt records: Pettway (Wilcox), Lyon (Marengo), King (Dallas), Pickens (Greene). These surnames appear in Carolina/Georgia records a generation earlier.
evidence.deed_records: Dallas, Marengo, and Greene county deed books document land purchases by planters whose previous addresses were in SC, GA, NC, and VA. Some deeds include bills of sale for enslaved people transported with the planter.

chain_mechanics.description: The planter migration to the Black Belt was the most dramatic forced migration in the antebellum South. Planters whose soil was exhausted in the Carolina Piedmont or Georgia cotton belt heard about the extraordinarily fertile Black Belt prairie — soil so rich that yields doubled what worn-out Carolina farms produced. They sold their eastern land, packed their households, and force-marched their enslaved laborers on foot to Alabama. The journey from central Georgia or the SC Piedmont to the Alabama Black Belt was 300-500 miles overland and took three to six weeks. Enslaved people walked in coffles — chained groups — driven by the planter or his agents. The trauma of the forced march, the severing of family and community ties in the origin state, and the arrival in a new and unfamiliar land were defining experiences for the enslaved population.
chain_mechanics.kinship_role: Among the planter class, kinship networks drove the migration: a brother or cousin who had already established a plantation in the Black Belt sent word back about the soil, the prices, and the opportunities. Among the enslaved, kinship ties were systematically broken — families separated at sale, communities shattered by the march. The enslaved people brought to the Black Belt were frequently from different plantations, different counties, and different states, forced to rebuild community from fragments.
chain_mechanics.timeline: Trickle 1817-1820 (immediately after Creek cession). First wave 1820-1830 (Carolina and Georgia planters). Second wave 1830-1840 (after Choctaw cession opened western counties). Slowing after 1840 as best land was claimed.

migration_corridors.dignity_note: The planter migration brought wealth to the Black Belt at the cost of human trafficking on a massive scale. Enslaved people did not 'move with' their enslavers — they were force-marched in chains across hundreds of miles, separated from families and communities, to labor on stolen land. This migration was the domestic slave trade in action.
migration_corridors.narrative_hook: When {ancestor_name} arrived in {county} County in the {decade}s, {pronoun_subject} did not choose to come — {pronoun_subject} was marched from {origin_county} in a coffle, weeks on foot through the backcountry, to labor on dark prairie soil that the Creek had been forced from a few years before.
migration_corridors.source: Tadman, Michael. Speculators and Slaves: Masters, Traders, and Slaves in the Old South. University of Wisconsin Press, 1989. Johnson, Walter. Soul by Soul: Life Inside the Antebellum Slave Market. Harvard University Press, 1999.

migration_corridors.corridor_name: The Domestic Slave Trade via Alabama River
migration_corridors.destination_area: Selma (Dallas Co.) as the primary Black Belt slave market; distribution to all eight counties
migration_corridors.scale: Tens of thousands of enslaved people were sold into the Alabama Black Belt between 1820 and 1860. The domestic slave trade was the largest forced migration in North American history, and the Black Belt was one of its primary destinations. Selma was a major slave-trading center — traders maintained offices on Broad Street and auctioned enslaved people at the courthouse steps.

evidence.court_records: Dallas County court records include probate inventories listing enslaved individuals by name, age, and value. Bills of sale document the purchase of enslaved people from Upper South traders.
evidence.newspaper_evidence: Selma newspapers published advertisements for slave sales, including descriptions of individuals, ages, and skills. Runaway advertisements provide physical descriptions and often name the state of origin.
evidence.trader_records: Slave trader correspondence and account books, where they survive, document the movement of enslaved people from Virginia and Maryland to the Alabama market. The Isaac Franklin and John Armfield firm, the largest domestic slave traders, operated extensively in the Alabama market.

chain_mechanics.description: The domestic slave trade operated through two channels: overland coffles and river/coastal transport. Overland coffles marched enslaved people from Virginia, Maryland, and the Carolinas through Georgia to Alabama — a journey of weeks. The coastal-river route shipped enslaved people by sea from the Chesapeake to New Orleans or Mobile, then upriver by steamboat to Selma and other Black Belt landings. Professional slave traders — men like Isaac Franklin and John Armfield — bought enslaved people in the Upper South where tobacco agriculture was declining and sold them in the Lower South where cotton demand was insatiable. The trade systematically separated families: children sold from parents, spouses sold apart, siblings scattered across states. The Black Belt's enslaved population was assembled from fragments of destroyed families.
chain_mechanics.kinship_role: The domestic slave trade was the deliberate destruction of kinship. Enslaved people arriving in the Black Belt had been ripped from family networks in Virginia, Maryland, the Carolinas, and beyond. The formation of new kinship networks in the Black Belt — marriages across plantation lines, fictive kin, church communities — was an act of reconstruction in the face of systematic destruction.
chain_mechanics.timeline: Continuous from 1820 to 1860, peaking in the 1830s and 1850s when cotton prices were highest.

migration_corridors.dignity_note: The domestic slave trade was not a market abstraction. It was the sale of human beings on courthouse steps, the separation of mothers from children, the coffle march of chained people across hundreds of miles. Name the people where records permit. The trade was not 'labor migration' — it was human trafficking enforced by law.
migration_corridors.narrative_hook: {ancestor_name} did not arrive in the Black Belt by choice — {pronoun_subject} was sold at Selma or arrived in a coffle from Virginia, separated from {pronoun_possessive} family by a transaction recorded in the courthouse as casually as a land deed.
migration_corridors.source: Tadman, Speculators and Slaves (1989). Johnson, Soul by Soul (1999). Deyle, Steven. Carry Me Back: The Domestic Slave Trade in American Life. Oxford University Press, 2005.

migration_corridors.corridor_name: Vine and Olive Colony: Napoleonic French Exiles at Demopolis
migration_corridors.scale: Approximately 150-400 French settlers arrived between 1817 and the early 1820s. Most abandoned the colony within a decade. The colony was a failure as an agricultural enterprise — French settlers had no experience with frontier agriculture, the vine and olive crops did not thrive, and many colonists lacked practical skills. Those who remained adapted by acquiring enslaved labor and growing cotton, becoming planters indistinguishable from their American neighbors.

evidence.congressional_records: Congressional act of 1817 granting the French colonists land in the Tombigbee region. Land office records document the original grants.
evidence.census_evidence: 1820 and 1830 census for Marengo County shows French-surnamed household heads (Lefebvre, Labuzan, Ravesies). By 1840, most had either left or assimilated.
evidence.church_evidence: Early Catholic records in Demopolis (the only Catholic community in the Black Belt) document French families.

chain_mechanics.description: The Vine and Olive Colony was organized in Philadelphia by French Napoleonic exiles who petitioned Congress for a land grant. Congress authorized a grant of four townships along the Tombigbee River. The settlers were a mix of former military officers, civilians, and adventurers — few had agricultural experience. The colony struggled from the start: the land grant was in unsurveyed territory, the soil did not suit vineyards, and frontier conditions were incompatible with the settlers' urban backgrounds. Within a decade, most French settlers had left for New Orleans, Mobile, or France. Those who stayed abandoned viticulture for cotton and assimilated into the planter class.
chain_mechanics.kinship_role: The French community in Demopolis maintained cultural cohesion for a generation through language, Catholic worship, and intermarriage. By the 1840s, assimilation was nearly complete.
chain_mechanics.timeline: Colony established 1817. Peak population early 1820s. Rapid decline by late 1820s. French cultural remnants persisted through the antebellum period.

migration_corridors.narrative_hook: The Vine and Olive Colony at Demopolis was a dream that the prairie soil rejected — {ancestor_name}'s French forebears came to grow grapes in a country that grew cotton, and the prairie won the argument within a decade.
migration_corridors.source: Rogers et al., Alabama: The History of a Deep South State (1994). Napier, John Hawkins, III. 'The Vine and Olive Colony.' Alabama Heritage, no. 58 (2000).

migration_corridors.corridor_name: Black Great Migration from the AL Black Belt
migration_corridors.destination_area: Birmingham (first stop for many); then Chicago, Detroit, Cleveland, Pittsburgh, Gary (IN), Milwaukee, St. Louis — via railroad north through Birmingham and the L&N corridor
migration_corridors.scale: The Black Belt's Black population declined in every census decade from 1910 to 1970. The outmigration was catastrophic in scale: Lowndes County's population fell from over 26,000 in 1900 to under 15,000 by 1960. Greene County, Sumter County, Wilcox County, and Hale County experienced similar losses. The cumulative effect was the depopulation of the region that had the highest concentration of Black Americans in the country. Tens of thousands of families left across five decades.

evidence.census_evidence: 1920-1960 census for Chicago, Detroit, and Cleveland shows dense concentrations of Alabama-born Black residents. Many specifically list Black Belt county birthplaces. Federal census for the Black Belt counties shows steady population decline despite high birth rates — the difference is outmigration.
evidence.church_evidence: Black Baptist and AME church rolls in the Black Belt show declining membership. Destination churches in northern cities show 'received by letter' entries from Alabama congregations. Some Black Belt churches closed entirely as the congregation left.
evidence.railroad_records: The Louisville & Nashville Railroad and the Southern Railway carried the migration. Birmingham was the typical first stop — close enough to return if the northern destination did not work out. From Birmingham, the Illinois Central and L&N carried families north to Chicago and Detroit.

chain_mechanics.description: The Great Migration from the Black Belt followed kinship and church networks with military precision. The typical pattern: a young man left first — train to Birmingham, work in the mines or steel mills for a few months, then north to Chicago or Detroit where a cousin, church member, or former neighbor had already established a foothold. Once settled, he sent word back — sometimes money for a train ticket, sometimes just information about jobs and housing. The wife and children followed. Then the siblings, the parents, the in-laws. Entire church congregations reassembled in northern neighborhoods. The push factors were overwhelming: sharecropping poverty, boll weevil destruction, racial violence, disenfranchisement, and the total absence of economic opportunity. The pull was industrial wages that exceeded anything the Black Belt had ever paid — even the worst northern factory job paid more in a week than a sharecropper cleared in a year.
chain_mechanics.kinship_role: Kinship and church networks were everything. A family did not migrate into a void — they migrated to a specific address, a specific church, a specific block where someone they knew was already living. The density of these networks meant that entire Black Belt communities reconstituted themselves in specific northern neighborhoods: Dallas County families in specific Chicago South Side blocks, Marengo County families in specific Detroit neighborhoods.
chain_mechanics.timeline: Trickle 1910-1916. First wave 1917-1920 (WWI industrial demand). Sustained 1920-1930 (boll weevil devastation). Depression slowdown 1930-1940 (fewer northern jobs). Second wave 1942-1955 (WWII and postwar industrial boom). Continuing 1955-1970.

migration_corridors.narrative_hook: When {ancestor_name} left {county} County for Birmingham and then Chicago in {year}, {pronoun_subject} was not the first — a cousin had gone the year before, and a church deacon had sent word about the packinghouse that was hiring. The Black Belt lost its people the same way it had gained them: in chains of connection, one family pulling the next.
migration_corridors.source: Grossman, James R. Land of Hope: Chicago, Black Southerners, and the Great Migration. University of Chicago Press, 1989. Wilkerson, Isabel. The Warmth of Other Suns. Random House, 2010. Gregory, James N. The Southern Diaspora: How the Great Migrations of Black and White Southerners Transformed America. University of North Carolina Press, 2005.

migration_corridors.corridor_name: White Rural Depopulation: Yeoman and Small Farmer Out-Migration
migration_corridors.destination_area: Birmingham, Tuscaloosa, Montgomery — Alabama cities offering industrial and service employment
migration_corridors.scale: Moderate but steady. The white population of the hill margins and smaller farms in the Black Belt declined as agricultural opportunities disappeared. This migration received far less attention than the Black Great Migration but was equally driven by economic collapse. By 1960, the Black Belt's white population was concentrated in county seats; the rural hinterland was increasingly abandoned.

evidence.census_evidence: Census records for Black Belt counties show declining white rural population alongside declining Black population. White families moved to county seats or to Birmingham, Tuscaloosa, and Montgomery.

chain_mechanics.description: White small farmers and yeoman families in the Black Belt's hill margins faced the same agricultural collapse as Black sharecroppers — exhausted soil, boll weevil, and no replacement economy. Those with education or capital moved to county seats or Alabama cities. Those without capital often remained in declining rural communities. The migration was less dramatic than the Black Great Migration because white families had more options closer to home — Birmingham's industrial economy and Tuscaloosa's university economy were within a day's drive.
chain_mechanics.kinship_role: Family networks facilitated the move to cities, similar to the Black migration pattern but operating within a shorter geographic radius.
chain_mechanics.timeline: Gradual throughout the 1900-1960 period, accelerating after WWII.

migration_corridors.narrative_hook: The Black Belt lost its white yeoman families too — {ancestor_name} left {county} County for Birmingham not because of racial oppression but because the cotton played out and the dark prairie soil that had grown fortunes for the planters offered nothing to a small farmer working forty acres with a mule.
migration_corridors.source: Rogers et al., Alabama: The History of a Deep South State (1994). Flynt, Wayne. Poor but Proud: Alabama's Poor Whites. University of Alabama Press, 1989.

internal_movement_patterns.pattern_name: Plantation to Sharecropper Cabin: Emancipation Without Migration
internal_movement_patterns.description: The most common 'migration' in the Black Belt after 1865 was no migration at all — formerly enslaved families remained on the same plantation, working the same soil, but now as sharecroppers instead of enslaved laborers. The Freedmen's Bureau labor contracts of 1865-1867 often formalized arrangements on the same plantation where the workers had been enslaved. A family that appears in the 1870 census on a former plantation was not there by free choice in any meaningful sense — the absence of land, capital, and alternatives kept them in place as surely as the chains had.
internal_movement_patterns.narrative_hook: {ancestor_name} was free but did not leave — because freedom without land, without capital, without anywhere to go meant staying on the same soil, in a different cabin, working the same cotton under a different name for the same result.

internal_movement_patterns.pattern_name: Gee's Bend: Isolation as Community Survival
internal_movement_patterns.description: Gee's Bend in Wilcox County — a community in a horseshoe bend of the Alabama River — represents a unique internal pattern: a community that stayed in place for over a century, from enslavement on the Pettway plantation through sharecropping, New Deal resettlement, and the civil rights era. The community's geographic isolation (accessible primarily by ferry) preserved cultural traditions (quilting, music, religious practice) that were disrupted in more connected communities. When the ferry was closed in the early 1960s to punish voter registration activity, the isolation that had been a cultural preserver became a tool of oppression.
internal_movement_patterns.narrative_hook: Gee's Bend was a place the world forgot and the people remembered — {ancestor_name}'s community kept quilting patterns, songs, and stories that the Great Migration scattered everywhere else, preserved by the same isolation that white authorities later used as a punishment.

settlement_clusters.description: Selma's Black community was the largest urban Black population in the Black Belt — freedpeople, artisans, laborers, teachers, and ministers who formed the institutional base for the civil rights movement. Selma University (Baptist, founded 1878) and the Black church network provided the organizational infrastructure.

settlement_clusters.cluster_name: Chicago South Side — Black Belt Alabama transplants
counties: Destination cluster — multiple Black Belt origin counties
settlement_clusters.description: Black Belt families from Dallas, Marengo, and surrounding counties concentrated in specific Chicago South Side neighborhoods. Churches, social clubs, and neighborhood associations organized around Alabama county-of-origin identity. These communities maintained Black Belt cultural practices — foodways, church traditions, quilting — in a northern industrial setting.

settlement_clusters.cluster_name: Birmingham Black community — Black Belt feeder
counties: Destination cluster — all eight Black Belt counties
settlement_clusters.description: Birmingham's Black community was the first-stop destination for most Black Belt out-migrants. Some stayed permanently; many used Birmingham as a staging point for the journey north. The Smithfield, Enon Ridge, and Titusville neighborhoods had significant Black Belt-origin populations. Birmingham's industrial economy — iron, steel, mining — offered wages unimaginable in the sharecropping counties.

The Gee's Bend community — descendants of enslaved people on the Pettway plantation — remained in the Alabama River horseshoe bend for over a century. The community's continuity, isolation, and cultural production (especially the quilting tradition, now internationally recognized) make it one of the most documented rural Black communities in the South.

narrative_hooks: The Black Belt was populated by three forced migrations — the Creek removed, the enslaved imported, and a century later the descendants of the enslaved driven north by poverty. {ancestor_name} was part of one of these movements, and none of them were free choices.

The domestic slave trade brought {ancestor_name} to {county} County from {origin_state} — marched in a coffle or shipped by river, sold at Selma or on a plantation porch, and put to work on soil that the Creek had been farming five years earlier.

When {ancestor_name} left the Black Belt for Chicago in {year}, {pronoun_subject} followed a path marked by kin and church — a cousin on the South Side who knew where the jobs were, a deacon who had left the year before and said there was room.
Gee's Bend was the Black Belt in miniature — enslaved, emancipated, sharecropped, isolated, and finally visible when the quilts reached the museums and the ferry returned.

The Black Belt lost more people to the Great Migration than it had gained in the antebellum slave trade — the same dark soil that had attracted the planters could not hold the descendants of the people the planters had enslaved.
source_references: Tadman, Michael. Speculators and Slaves: Masters, Traders, and Slaves in the Old South. Madison: University of Wisconsin Press, 1989.
Johnson, Walter. Soul by Soul: Life Inside the Antebellum Slave Market. Cambridge: Harvard University Press, 1999.
Deyle, Steven. Carry Me Back: The Domestic Slave Trade in American Life. New York: Oxford University Press, 2005.
Grossman, James R. Land of Hope: Chicago, Black Southerners, and the Great Migration. Chicago: University of Chicago Press, 1989.
Wilkerson, Isabel. The Warmth of Other Suns: The Epic Story of America's Great Migration. New York: Random House, 2010.
Gregory, James N. The Southern Diaspora: How the Great Migrations of Black and White Southerners Transformed America. Chapel Hill: University of North Carolina Press, 2005.
Ethridge, Robbie. Creek Country: The Creek Indians and Their World. Chapel Hill: University of North Carolina Press, 2003.
Rogers, William Warren, Robert David Ward, Leah Rawls Atkins, and Wayne Flynt. Alabama: The History of a Deep South State. Tuscaloosa: University of Alabama Press, 1994.
Flynt, Wayne. Poor but Proud: Alabama's Poor Whites. Tuscaloosa: University of Alabama Press, 1989.
